indexContent:
# Architecture Decision Records

> Reconstructed from [`django/django`](https://github.com/django/django) by ADR Archaeologist.

| ID | Title | Status | Category | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ADR-001 | Use Django ORM as the primary database abstraction layer | accepted | database | 97% |
| ADR-002 | Implement a pluggable authentication backend system | accepted | auth | 93% |
| ADR-003 | Use a URL dispatcher with regex/path-based routing | accepted | patterns | 95% |

adrs.title: Use Django ORM as the primary database abstraction layer
adrs.context: The Django framework needed a database abstraction layer that would allow developers to interact with relational databases without writing raw SQL. The team required a solution that could support multiple database backends while providing a Pythonic API that matched the framework's philosophy of making common tasks easy.
adrs.decision: Django adopted an Object-Relational Mapper (ORM) as the primary interface between Python application code and the underlying relational database, with raw SQL access available as an escape hatch.

positive: Enables rapid development through a Pythonic API
Provides database portability across PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and Oracle
negative: Introduces a performance overhead for complex queries
Requires developers to understand both the ORM API and the SQL it generates
Occasionally produces suboptimal queries that require manual optimization

alternatives.option: Raw SQL with a thin connection wrapper
alternatives.reason: Rejected — would sacrifice database portability and a Pythonic developer experience
Rejected — an external dependency misaligned with the framework's batteries-included philosophy
Rejected — incompatible with the relational data model Django targets

archaeology.rejectionReason: Early migration files show the ORM was designed to manage schema state from the beginning, indicating raw SQL management was explicitly rejected.

adrs.markdownContent:
# ADR-001: Use Django ORM as the primary database abstraction layer

Date: 2005-07-13
Status: Accepted
Category: database
Confidence: 97%

## Context

The Django framework needed a database abstraction layer that would allow developers to interact with relational databases without writing raw SQL.

## Decision

Django adopted an Object-Relational Mapper (ORM) as the primary interface between Python application code and the underlying relational database.

## Consequences

### Positive
- Enables rapid development through a Pythonic API
- Provides database portability across PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and Oracle

### Negative
- Introduces a performance overhead for complex queries
- Occasionally produces suboptimal queries that require manual optimization

adrs.title: Implement a pluggable authentication backend system
adrs.context: Django needed an authentication system flexible enough to support diverse deployment scenarios — from simple username/password authentication to LDAP, OAuth, and custom enterprise identity providers. A hardcoded authentication implementation would have prevented adoption in enterprise environments.
adrs.decision: Django implemented a pluggable authentication backend system where authentication logic is delegated to a list of backend classes, each implementing a standard interface. The default backend authenticates against the built-in User model, but any number of custom backends can be added.

positive: Usable in virtually any authentication context without modifying core code
Supports both authentication and authorization through one backend interface
negative: Adds complexity to understanding the authentication flow
Requires careful ordering of backends in the AUTHENTICATION_BACKENDS setting

alternatives.option: Hardcoded username/password authentication against the User model
alternatives.reason: Rejected — would prevent adoption in enterprise identity environments
Rejected — insufficient for chaining multiple identity providers
Rejected — would couple authentication to the request pipeline and reduce reusability

adrs.markdownContent:
# ADR-002: Implement a pluggable authentication backend system

Date: 2006-05-02
Status: Accepted
Category: auth
Confidence: 93%

## Context

Django needed an authentication system flexible enough to support diverse deployment scenarios.

## Decision

Django implemented a pluggable authentication backend system where authentication logic is delegated to a list of backend classes.

## Consequences

### Positive
- Usable in virtually any authentication context without modifying core code

### Negative
- Adds complexity to understanding the authentication flow

adrs.title: Use a URL dispatcher with regex/path-based routing
adrs.context: Django needed a URL routing mechanism that was explicit, readable, and decoupled from the view logic. The framework's philosophy of explicit over implicit required that URL patterns be defined in a central location rather than inferred from file structure or decorators.
adrs.decision: Django adopted a centralized URL dispatcher where URL patterns are defined as a list of path() or re_path() entries in urls.py files, mapping URL patterns to view functions or class-based views.

positive: Makes the full URL structure of an application visible in one place
Prevents magic routing behavior through explicit mapping
negative: Requires maintaining a separate urls.py file
Can become unwieldy in large applications without careful use of include()

alternatives.option: File-system-based routing (URL derived from file path)
alternatives.reason: Rejected — implicit routing conflicts with Django's explicit-over-implicit philosophy
Rejected — scatters URL structure across the codebase, harming discoverability
Rejected — magic conventions reduce clarity for large applications

archaeology.rejectionReason: The coexistence of path() and re_path() indicates a migration away from regex-only routing, partially superseding the original approach.

adrs.markdownContent:
# ADR-003: Use a URL dispatcher with regex/path-based routing

Date: 2005-07-13
Status: Accepted
Category: patterns
Confidence: 95%

## Context

Django needed a URL routing mechanism that was explicit, readable, and decoupled from the view logic.

## Decision

Django adopted a centralized URL dispatcher where URL patterns are defined as a list of path() or re_path() entries in urls.py files.

## Consequences

### Positive
- Makes the full URL structure of an application visible in one place

### Negative
- Can become unwieldy in large applications without careful use of include()
